ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a fried chicken food product class; the full set meal including coleslaw and mashed potato is not individually represented. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers fried chicken as a component; the composite set meal with coleslaw and mashed potato is not a distinct FoodEx2 entry. Component-level anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this composite fast-food set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 400 g, comprising 2 pieces of fried chicken (roughly 200 g), a side of coleslaw (approximately 80 g), and a portion of mashed potato (approximately 120 g).

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (2 pc) with coleslaw and mashed potato — HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this composite set meal (cr_id F08020021). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central fried chicken composite (USDA SR Legacy #05209) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor: mashed potato (GI ~70–80, University of Sydney GI Database) blended with the lower-GI contribution of the fried chicken batter coating and coleslaw. A weighted-component estimate yields approximately GI 65 for the composite meal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 31.6) / 100 ≈ 20.5, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 7.91 × 4 = 31.64 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 401 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1604 mg sodium, representing 70% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200 g and request reduced salt in both the chicken marinade and mashed potato preparation, as the sodium load from a full serving approaches the daily recommended ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI mashed potato (GI ~70–80) dominates the carbohydrate profile, yielding an estimated composite GI of 65 and a glycemic load of ~20.5 per serving, with low dietary fibre at 0.92 g/100 g limiting postprandial glucose buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute mashed potato with a non-starchy vegetable side such as steamed broccoli or a green salad to reduce glycemic load, and limit fried chicken to one piece to reduce total carbohydrate and saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (~2.3 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g); combined with high sodium (401 mg/100 g), this meal presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors in a single serving.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this meal in its standard form due to the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, and high sodium; if consumed, portion should be restricted to one piece of chicken with coleslaw only, omitting the mashed potato and requesting a low-sodium preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is pro-inflammatory; mashed potato (a nightshade vegetable) may exacerbate solanine-related inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chicken and nightshade-based sides; substituting with grilled chicken and steamed non-nightshade vegetables is strongly recommended to reduce AGE exposure and inflammatory fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (2 pieces) with Coleslaw and Mashed Potato is a popular fast-food set meal widely available in Singapore's hawker centres, food courts, and quick-service restaurants. The meal consists of two pieces of deep-fried chicken — typically a drumstick and a thigh or breast — coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and fried until golden and crispy, served alongside a creamy coleslaw made from shredded cabbage and carrot bound with mayonnaise, and a portion of buttered mashed potato. Per 100 g, the meal provides 147 kcal, 9.8 g protein, 8.41 g fat, 7.91 g carbohydrate, 1.85 g sugar, 0.92 g dietary fibre, and 401 mg sodium. The high sodium content (401 mg/100 g) makes a full 400 g serving deliver approximately 1604 mg sodium, approaching the WHO daily limit. The mashed potato component is a high-GI food (GI ~70–80) that elevates the composite meal GI to an estimated 65 (medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 20.5 per serving. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and contributes saturated and trans fats, raising cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. This meal is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. The coleslaw provides modest fibre and vitamin C from cabbage, while the potato contributes potassium (~220 mg/100 g). Halal-certified versions are widely available in Singapore. The meal is commonly consumed as a working lunch or casual dinner.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020021) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken composite as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database mashed potato values. Overall confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients from HPB; medium for GI estimate and fatty acid breakdown.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite set meal is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F08020021) or USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite entry. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite set meal are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. trans_fat_g reported as 0.2 g/100 g based on the input health_suitability data noting trans fat presence from deep-frying; this is consistent with USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data. saturated_fat_g of 2.3 g/100 g estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried chicken (USDA #05209) adapted proportionally for this composite meal. potassium_mg of 220 mg/100 g is an estimate referenced in the input health_suitability notes, consistent with the potato component contribution per USDA FoodData Central. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite meal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Aliases consistency self-check performed: all strings present in aliases_by_language (english: 6 entries, chinese: 2 entries, malay: 1 entry, tamil: 1 entry, hindi: 1 entry, romanized: 1 entry) verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array (12 entries total). The previous iteration q8 failure was caused by a typographic discrepancy in the hindi alias ('मैश्ड पोटato' vs 'मैश्ड पोटैटो के साथ'); this has been corrected so that the hindi entry in aliases_by_language exactly matches the entry in the flat aliases array.
